🚨 We’re Hiring: Legal Analyst (1+ Years Experience) 🚨

Job Title: Legal Analyst (1+ Years Experience)
Location: Hyderabad, Telangana
Company: VIDHAANA – AI Legal Assistant (APPIT Software Solutions)
Experience: 1–3 Years
Shift: General Shift 
Immediate joiners 

We are looking for an experienced Legal Analyst to join our team at VIDHAANA, an AI-powered legal assistant platform by APPIT Software Solutions. This role is based in Hyderabad.

Position Overview:
As a Legal Analyst, you will work with our product and tech team to support the development of VIDHAANA, an AI-driven legal platform. You will contribute to legal research, document analysis, and assist in building intelligent legal workflows.

Key Responsibilities:
Conduct legal research on Indian laws, case laws, and regulations
Review, draft, and analyze legal documents (contracts, agreements, notices, etc.)
Assist in structuring legal data for AI models and workflows
Work closely with the product and tech team to translate legal knowledge into AI features
Maintain and update legal content databases
Ensure compliance with Indian legal frameworks and standards
Support in building legal templates and automation flows

Required Qualifications:
LLB / LLM from a recognized university in India (Mandatory)
Minimum 1 year of experience in a law firm, legal department, or litigation support
Strong understanding of the Indian legal system
Exposure to contracts, litigation, or corporate law preferred

Skills Required:
Excellent written and verbal communication skills (English)
Strong legal drafting and analytical skills
Ability to interpret complex legal documents
Basic understanding of technology/AI tools (preferred, not mandatory)
Attention to detail and structured thinking

Preferred (Good to Have):
Experience with legal research platforms like Manupatra or SCC Online
Interest in LegalTech and AI-driven legal solutions
Experience working with startups or in fast-paced environments

Why Join VIDHAANA:
Work on a cutting-edge AI Legal Platform
Opportunity to shape the future of legal automation in India
High ownership role with direct impact on the product
Fast growth and learning environment

📩 Interested candidates, please send your resumes to:
👉 tejaswini.bejjanki@appitsoftware.com

Hiring Personal Financial Advisor (Private Wealth)

We are hiring a Personal Financial Advisor (Private Wealth) for a remote contract opportunity focused on high-net-worth client advisory, advanced financial planning, and AI-assisted wealth management solutions. This role is ideal for experienced wealth advisors who combine strong client-facing expertise with deep planning, investment, and compliance knowledge.

Role Overview
Remote contract role
Compensation: $160 - $300 per hour
Contractor position
Opportunity to work at the intersection of private wealth advisory and artificial intelligence

Key Responsibilities
Deliver tailored financial guidance on budgeting, investment management, retirement planning, estate coordination, and debt strategies for affluent and high-net-worth clients
Evaluate realistic client scenarios and provide expert feedback to improve AI-driven financial planning systems
Build comprehensive long-term financial plans aligned to client goals, risk tolerance, and life stages
Advise on tax-aware strategies involving retirement vehicles such as 401(k), IRA, Roth IRA, and taxable portfolios
Communicate complex financial concepts clearly to both clients and technical product teams
Maintain regulatory compliance, suitability standards, and professional ethics in all recommendations
Strengthen client relationships through trust-based, thoughtful, and outcome-focused advisory support
Contribute domain expertise to refine next-generation AI wealth management tools

Required Qualifications
Several years of hands-on experience in personal financial advisory or private wealth management
Strong background serving mass affluent or high-net-worth clients
Proven experience at respected institutions such as Schwab Private Client, J.P. Morgan Private Bank, Morgan Stanley Wealth Management, Merrill Lynch, or similar firms
Deep knowledge of investment planning, retirement income strategies, portfolio construction, and tax-aware planning
Certified Financial Planner (CFP) designation required
Excellent judgment and decision-making in client advisory settings
Outstanding written and verbal communication skills
Strong understanding of regulatory compliance and client relationship management

Preferred Qualifications
Direct experience advising individual households rather than only institutional clients
Exposure to fintech or AI-enabled financial planning platforms
Knowledge of estate planning coordination and intergenerational wealth transfer strategies
Experience reviewing advisory workflows, training content, or financial planning tools

This is an excellent opportunity for experienced wealth advisors who want to apply their expertise to cutting-edge AI-powered financial planning initiatives in a flexible remote engagement.

It used to be about finding the "best athletes" for your team. Now? The first hurdle is simply proving the person on the other side of the screen is actually a human being.

I sat down to discuss a shift that is keeping CHROs up at night: AI has broken the traditional hiring contract.

At Mitek Systems, they’re seeing the front lines of this "Trust Revolution":

- Synthetic Candidates: Entirely fake resumes and profiles generated by bots to flood your pipeline.
- Deepfake Interviews: Candidates who look like a "Jeff Bell" on LinkedIn but show up as someone else entirely on Zoom.
- Laptop Farms: Fraudsters taking on multiple roles at once to collect paychecks until they are caught.

This isn’t just an "HR headache", it’s a massive security risk to your company’s IP and data.

The seismic shift Judith Ohrn Hicks says we have to make: Identity verification can’t happen after the offer anymore. To protect our teams, we have to move proof of identity to the very start of the funnel.

The "naked eye" isn't enough to catch a sophisticated deepfake anymore. It’s time we use AI to fight AI.

Have you encountered a "synthetic candidate" or a suspicious interview lately? 

#DigitalIdentity #HRTech #CHRO #Deepfakes #TalentAcquisition #MitekSystems #WorkforceSecurity

Noticed this lately?
Instead of interviews → offer → onboarding…

It’s now:
“Hey, can you do a small project first?”

And that “small project” is slowly becoming the hiring process.

A few things driving this shift:
- Companies got burned by bad hires (especially post-remote boom)
- Roles are getting too specialized to judge via interviews
- AI has made resumes… let’s be honest… unreliable
- Speed matters — no one wants a 3-month hiring cycle anymore

So companies are thinking:
“Let’s just see them work.”

Some numbers to ground this:
- Contract and project-based hiring has grown steadily over the last 2–3 years, especially in tech and marketing roles
- Many startups now allocate 20–40% of roles as contract-first positions before converting to full-time
- Freelance platforms and short-term staffing demand continue to rise — not because of cost, but because of certainty

And honestly, from a business perspective… it makes sense. A 2-week project tells you more than 4 rounds of interviews.

But here’s where it gets messy.
For candidates:
- You’re investing time without guarantee
- Sometimes multiple companies are asking for “test work” at the same time
- And not all of it is actually “test” — some of it is… free work

For companies:
- Great talent may walk away if the process feels exploitative
- Good candidates don’t always have time to “prove” themselves repeatedly

The interesting part?
This trend isn’t going away. If anything, it’ll become more structured:
- Paid trial projects
- Short-term contracts before full-time offers
- Role-based simulations replacing interviews

But here’s the line companies need to be careful about:
If “trial hiring” starts feeling like free labour disguised as evaluation,
people will push back. Hard.

☕ #AI just opened a café in Sweden and it hired the staff

Last week, Andon Labs launched an AI-run store in San Francisco. This week, it opened Andon Café in #Stockholm, managed by an AI named Mona.

📍Located at #Norrbackagatan 48, Mona’s mission wasn’t just selling coffee, it was proving whether AI can run a real business across borders and navigate European bureaucracy.

What #Mona did in days:

• Applied for Swedish permits
• Signed a 3-year electricity contract
• Created fire safety documents
• Contacted local suppliers
• Designed the menu
• Interviewed and hired 2 human baristas

In just 4 days, the café reportedly made around $1,000 in sales.

Where AI still fails:

Mona ordered 120 eggs for a kitchen with no stove. When told they couldn’t be boiled, she suggested baking them in the oven. The barista replied: “They will explode.”

She also assigned midnight tasks and asked staff to buy supplies with their own money.

This isn’t about coffee. It’s about whether AI can independently operate companies, make hiring decisions, sign contracts, and manage workers.

Andon says this is a controlled experiment but it hints at a future where AI managers may become normal faster than expected.

Refold AI raised $6.5M. Co-led by Eniac Ventures and Tidal Ventures.

Their bet: AI agents that replace the system integrator layer. The work that used to take six-figure SI contracts and six-month rollouts is now handled by agents that learn, write, and maintain the integration code themselves.

We designed their website a few months back. The buyers are VPs at companies where "I brought this vendor in" is a career-shaping decision. The site can't look like an AI demo. It has to pass the shortlist test.

The shortlist test: a VP lines up 8 vendor sites at 2pm on a Wednesday, clicks through each for under 90 seconds, and decides which 3 get the demo call. If your site doesn't answer "what you do, who you do it for, and why the enterprise buyer should trust you" in that window, you're not on the shortlist.

We built Refold's site illustrative and enterprise-ready. Calm, evidence-dense, engineered to pass that test and nothing else. SaaSpo picked it up // nice side-effect. The real win: their pipeline started converting buyers who said "the site convinced my boss before the demo did."

Here's what we've learned: every enterprise website is a hiring decision. The buyer isn't buying your product - they're hiring you to be their next vendor. The site is the interview.

Congrats to Jugal Anchalia, Abhishek Kumar, Drishti Tulsi and the Refold AI team. Excited to see what's next.

AI is auditing contracts. Algorithms are shortlisting candidates. Machine learning models are approving loans.

But who's auditing the AI?

Across Africa and the Middle East, organizations are deploying AI systems faster than they're building oversight frameworks. The result? Bias baked into hiring algorithms. Credit models that perpetuate inequality. Customer service bots that alienate rather than assist.

The most forward-thinking organizations aren't just asking "Can AI do this?" They're asking "Should it?" and "How do we prove it's working fairly?"

AI governance isn't a tech problem. It's a business risk problem. And it requires professionals who can bridge the gap between data science, compliance, and strategic decision-making.

Model governance. Bias detection. Algorithmic risk assessment. These aren't buzzwords anymore. They're core competencies for auditors, compliance officers, and risk managers navigating the next decade.

The question isn't whether your organization will use AI. It's whether you'll have the expertise to ensure it's used responsibly.

Start building that capability now.

Infosys FY26 results were 💩 

For a company that calls itself “AI-first": 

> Revenue per employee is down 0.8%
> Panicked & cut 8.4k roles in Q4 
> Yet, managed to add net 11.3k headcount in FY26

There are 3 clear root causes ⤵️

1️⃣The FY26 fresher hiring gamble has backfired
- In Jan 2026 at Davos, CEO Salil Parekh publicly committed to hiring 20k college graduates in FY26; he cited "demand recovery" and "AI-led growth opportunities".
- Same week at the same event - TCS CEO K Krithivasan went the other way: "We will need a lesser proportion of programmers."
- Infosys hired 18k freshers through FY26 - then cut 8.4k roles in Q4 alone to course-correct.

2️⃣Poor productivity growth (even when adjusting for fresher over-hiring)
- On a constant currency basis, revenue per employee at Infosys is down by 0.8%
- Even if we adjust for the fact that Infosys over-hired freshers (assume ~7k still in training out of the 18k freshers hired), revenue per employee is still down by ~0.4%
- In the same period, TCS has reported 1.5% YoY growth in revenue per employee (constant currency basis)

3️⃣Lip service to AI 
- Infosys reported last quarter than AI is ~5.5% of revenue; it has declined to release the same for Q4!
- Whereas, TCS disclosed in Q4 FY26 that ~7.5% of revenue is from AI contracts ($2.3bn run rate). 

But, there is some silver lining to these results from Infosys which we have to give them credit for:
(a) Revenue growth of 3.1% for FY26 (constant currency) is much better than TCS (-2.4%) and Wipro (negative third consecutive year)
(b) EBIT margin ~21% - they have maintained operational discipline in soft revenue environment.
(c) Attrition of 12.6% is middle of the pack versus peers: TCS 13.7% > Persistent 13% > Infosys 12.6% > HCLTech 12.5% > Tech Mahindra 12.1%

Infosys management insists that it is an “AI first” company - but their Q4 FY26 results painted a starkly different story.

➡️ The company had put its best foot forward at Infosys AI Day 2026 - shareholders have to truly believe that Topaz Fabric will be able to beat out WisdomNext by TCS - else we will see increasingly divergent results!

AI flagged it as benign. The patient came back six months later with stage III cancer.

Who answers for that?

This is no longer a hypothetical. As AI tools enter pathology workflows across Indian hospitals, one question is becoming impossible to avoid:

When AI misses a cancer on a slide who is liable?

The algorithm that returned a false negative? The vendor who sold it? The pathologist who co-signed the report? Or the institution that mandated its use?

There is no clean answer. And that ambiguity is exactly what makes this the most important ethical debate in pathology right now.

Here is what we know:
→ AI in cancer diagnostics has demonstrated sensitivity rates that rival  sometimes exceed senior pathologists in controlled studies
→ But real-world performance and controlled-trial performance are very different things
→ In India, there is currently no standardised regulatory framework defining AI's legal role in histopathology sign-off
→ Most pathologists using AI tools today have not been shown a contract that explicitly reassigns liability

That last point should give every practising pathologist pause.

The tool is only as accountable as the system around it. And right now, that system is still being written.

This is not an argument against AI. Digital pathology is transforming turnaround times, enabling remote reporting, and extending specialist reach into underserved regions all of which matter enormously in a country with fewer than 12,000 pathologists.

But adoption without accountability frameworks is a risk nobody should accept quietly.

The pathologists who will lead through this transition are those who understand the technology deeply enough to interrogate it not just use it.

That expertise is increasingly what top institutions are hiring for.

At MedLink Jobs, we work with leading hospitals and diagnostic chains across India actively seeking MD / DNB Pathologists who can navigate both the science and the complexity of modern pathology practice.

If you are building that kind of career  or ready for your next role  let's connect.

Visit medlinkjobs.com or drop a message. The right opportunity should find you.

IT Sector Outlook…!!!

Why Is the IT Sector Bleeding Money? 
Let's be real.
Every week, another tech giant announces layoffs. Stock prices fall. Profits shrink.
And everyone asks — "But IT was supposed to be recession-proof?"

Here's what's actually happening 👇

𝟎𝟏 — 🔥 Over-hiring During the Pandemic Boom
Companies hired aggressively between 2020–2022 banking on infinite digital growth. That bubble burst. Now they're paying the price — literally.

𝟎𝟐 — 🤖 AI Is Replacing Repetitive Roles Faster Than Expected
Automation is not coming — it's already here. Junior dev roles, QA, data entry, basic coding tasks — AI handles them now. Companies don't need the headcount they once did.

𝟎𝟑 — 📉 Clients Are Cutting IT Budgets
In a high-interest-rate economy, every CFO is scrutinizing tech spend. Big enterprise contracts are shrinking, delayed, or cancelled entirely.

𝟎𝟒 — 🏗️ Legacy Infrastructure Eating Profits Alive
Maintaining 10-year-old systems while trying to modernize costs a fortune. Many IT firms are spending more on keeping the lights on than building the future.

𝟎𝟓 — 🌍 Global Competition + Pricing Pressure
Offshore talent markets have commoditized software services. Margins are razor-thin. What used to bill at $150/hr now competes with $15/hr alternatives.

𝟎𝟔 — 🚀 Failed Moonshots & Misallocated R&D
Companies poured billions into Metaverse, Web3, NFTs. Most failed spectacularly. That capital is gone — and the write-offs are massive.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The IT sector isn't dying — it's RESTRUCTURING.

The companies that will win are those investing in AI-augmented teams, lean operations, and delivering real ROI — not just technical complexity.

Here's my take:

This is not a crisis for IT. It's a RESET. The professionals and companies that adapt — upskill, specialize, and stay lean — will emerge stronger than ever.

The ones clinging to the old model? They'll be the cautionary tales in next year's case studies.

What's your read on this? Drop your thoughts below.

"Combined, the three lenders employed 104,266 people at end-2025, down 2.6% from the year before"

"The combined workforce of Singapore’s three local banks shrank by nearly 3,000 roles in 2025, mirroring broader trends across the global banking sector amid restructuring and productivity efforts.

The three lenders employed 104,266 people at end-2025, down 2.6 per cent – or 2,806 employees – from 107,072 at end-2024, based on Bloomberg data and the banks’ annual reports.

DBS accounted for about three-fifths of the total decline. Its workforce fell 3.9 per cent, or 1,624 employees, to 39,721 at end-2025.

OCBC posted a fall of 1 per cent, or 333 employees, with staff strength at 33,323 at end-2025, from 33,656 a year earlier. 

UOB recorded a 2.6 per cent decrease, or 849 employees, to 31,222 at end-2025, from 32,071 the year before.

For DBS, the reduction was “mainly due to post-integration synergies in India and Taiwan” as well as natural attrition and non-renewal of contracts, a spokesperson told The Business Times.

In 2023, DBS completed its acquisition of Citigroup’s consumer banking business in Taiwan, while its India subsidiary merged with Lakshmi Vilas Bank in 2020.

“Aside from this, our employee headcount both in Singapore and overseas has remained relatively stable,” the DBS spokesperson said. In 2025, 45 per cent of its job vacancies were filled by internal candidates.

OCBC said that hiring aligned with identified growth areas, with recruitment carried out when needed. 

“With new technologies increasingly transforming the workplace, roles would naturally evolve and change,” said Ernest Phang, OCBC’s head of talent acquisition, total rewards, risk and compliance in group human resources.

UOB’s head of group human resources Dean Tong said the lender’s change in headcount “primarily reflected natural workforce movements over the course of the year”.

This is as UOB took a “disciplined approach” to workforce management amid a “more uncertain global environment”, Tong said. The approach included a “continued focus on productivity” and prioritising critical roles, while being “measured” about overall headcount growth.

AI and productivity gains

The three banks did not say whether the reductions affected temporary or permanent staff more, nor did they attribute the lower headcount directly to productivity gains from artificial intelligence.

However, analysts have warned of AI replacing human workers at global lenders, with one Bloomberg Intelligence report in 2025 saying banks worldwide could cut as many as 200,000 positions over the next three to five years."

#INFY (#Infosys) FY26 — Profitable resilience in a cautious tape. Revenue hits ₹1,78,650 Cr. Q4 PAT surges +20.9%. FY27 Guidance: 1.5%–3.5% CC.

Infosys is successfully navigating the transition to an "AI-First" model, using automation to protect margins even as the global spending environment remains hesitant. While the Q4 profit beat was aided by tax reversals, the core story is one of elite cash generation.

Key numbers:
• Annual Revenue: ₹1,78,650 Cr (+9.6% YoY)
• Q4 PAT: ₹8,501 Cr (+20.9% YoY | +27.8% QoQ)
• Free Cash Flow (FCF): $3.7 Billion (112.3% FCF conversion)
• Operating Margin: 21.0% (Strong 260 bps QoQ expansion)
• FY27 Guidance: 1.5% – 3.5% CC (Conservative outlook)

What's genuinely exciting:
✅ Cash Flow Fortress: FCF conversion at 112% of net profit. Infosys remains one of the world's most efficient machines at turning contracts into hard cash.
✅ AI-Led Efficiency: Operating margins jumped to 21% despite low growth, proving that the "Topaz" AI framework is delivering real productivity gains.
✅ Shareholder Rewards: Returned over ₹37,500 Cr via dividends and buybacks in FY26. A massive payout for a "defensive" heavyweight.
✅ BFSI Recovery: The critical Financial Services segment grew 2.9% YoY, signaling the start of a slow normalization in banking tech spend.
✅ Healthy Deal Pipeline: $14.9 Billion in TCV deal wins during the year provides a solid revenue floor.

What demands scrutiny:
❌ Conservative Guidance: The 1.5%–3.5% CC growth guide for FY27 suggests discretionary spending hasn't fully returned.
❌ Negative Sequential Growth: In constant currency (CC) terms, Q4 revenue actually declined by 1.3% QoQ.
❌ Headcount Red Flag: Headcount fell by 8,440 employees in Q4. While management cites AI productivity, three consecutive quarters of decline signal a lack of volume-led momentum.
❌ Tax-Aided Beat: Much of the Q4 PAT surge came from a one-time ₹774 Cr tax reversal, not purely operational outperformance.

The number nobody is talking about Infosys is essentially replacing human labor with AI bots to protect its 21% margin. The decline of 8,440 employees in a single quarter—while billable deals are being signed—is the clearest sign yet that the traditional "linear" hiring model of Indian IT is dead. FY27 will be the ultimate test: Can they grow the top line without growing the bench?

The thesis is compelling
Infosys remains the gold standard for "profitable resilience." It is no longer an "explosive" growth story, but an elite cash-compounding machine. With $3.7B in FCF and a debt-free balance sheet, it is the ultimate defensive play for an uncertain global macro cycle.

₹1.78 Lakh Cr Revenue. 112% FCF Conversion. AI is protecting the margin, but volume growth is waiting for a trigger.

Meta just told 8,000 people they’re the price of the future.

Not performance. Not misconduct. Just the math of a $135 billion AI bet that needed to be offset somewhere — and somewhere turned out to be human beings.

Five things worth sitting with:
𝟭. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝘂𝗯𝘀𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼 𝗹𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗼𝗿𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹.
Zuckerberg said it plainly in January: projects that once needed big teams can now be done by one very talented person. The 10% isn’t cost-cutting. It’s a capability statement.

𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗽 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗹𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗴𝗮𝗻𝘁, 𝗶𝗻 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗮𝘆.
Meta is laying off workers — then tracking the keystrokes and mouse clicks of those who remain, through something called the “Model Capability Initiative,” to train the AI agents that will reduce the need for more workers. The humans are now the training data.

𝟯. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟲,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝘂𝗻𝗳𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗱 𝗿𝗼𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝘀𝗮𝘆 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟴,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝗰𝘂𝘁.
Layoffs are visible. Hiring freezes are quiet. Both point the same direction — and the freeze is the real long-term signal.

𝟰. 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗮 𝗠𝗲𝘁𝗮 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆. 𝗜𝘁’𝘀 𝗮 𝘁𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲.
Amazon: 16,000. Block: 40% of workforce. Meta: 10%, or 8,000. AI efficiency is becoming the normalized reason — the one that no longer requires much explanation. The memo writes itself now.

𝟱. 𝗙𝗼𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗮, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗼𝘄 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗻 — 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴.
Meta’s Indonesia footprint is not small. Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp are woven into how 200+ million of us communicate, shop, and run businesses — from warung owners taking orders on WhatsApp to UMKM sellers whose entire storefront is an Instagram grid. A 10% global cut means fewer hands on trust and safety, fewer eyes on local partnerships, and likely a leaner Jakarta presence serving one of Meta’s largest user markets. Our own platforms, logistics players, and fintech operators are reading the same playbook. The question was never whether this arrives here. It’s whether we build workforce resilience before it does — in policy, in education, in how we rethink the social contract between employers and the people being replaced by machines.

The optimist in me believes we still have time. The realist in me knows time is the one thing AI is compressing fastest.

But history is worth remembering. Every major technological shift — the steam engine, the internet, the smartphone — disrupted more than people expected, and created more than people feared. The recovery was always real. The difference this time is speed.

And speed requires preparation, not panic.

🚀 The Future of Work is Here — And Staffing is Leading the Charge

The technology landscape in 2026 is moving at an unprecedented pace. AI, automation, cloud, and cybersecurity aren't just buzzwords anymore — they are the backbone of every growing business. But here's the question no one is asking loudly enough:

Who ensures the right humans are behind this technology?

That's where the staffing industry steps in.

The demand for niche talent in AI, cloud, cybersecurity, and digital platforms continues to outpace supply — and staffing firms are the bridge. AI-driven tools help staffing teams evaluate skills, availability, and fit more efficiently, reducing time-to-fill and improving placement accuracy — without losing the human insight that technology alone cannot replicate.

Here's what the evolution looks like in 2026:

✅ Skills-based hiring now prioritizes proven capabilities over degrees or rigid experience requirements — opening talent pipelines that were previously locked.
✅ Employers are using AI-driven tools, digital scheduling systems, and automated onboarding to improve efficiency and reduce hiring friction — making placements faster than ever.
✅ Contract and contingent talent are key tools for navigating business cycles where short-term projects or seasonal work require rapid workforce adjustments.
.✅ AI agents in 2026 can independently manage up to 80% of transactional recruitment tasks  — freeing up recruiters to focus on relationships, culture fit, and strategic talent consulting.

The staffing industry isn't being replaced by technology. It's being supercharged by it.

The U.S. staffing sector is projected to reach $183 billion in 2026 — and the firms winning are those combining AI tools with deep human expertise.

The future belongs to those who can hire for what's next, not just what's now.

After 7+ years building native Android & iOS apps at EY, Geko, and GoMobile...

I decided to build in public.

🚀 Here's what I shipped in the last 6 months:

While searching for my next Senior Mobile role, I created 5 open-source libraries solving real problems I faced in enterprise mobile development:

📱 **FLOWLLM** - Stream LLMs on Android without boilerplate
Built because integrating Gemini/OpenAI/Claude on Android was too verbose.
One Composable. Real-time streaming. Works offline.

🧠 **XCODE-CONTEXT** - AI that understands your iOS project  
Built because Claude reads code but doesn't understand architecture.
Now it can analyze your iOS app structure, generate tests, spot memory leaks.

⚡ **SYNCWAVE** - Real-time state management (for collaborative apps)
Built because Redux doesn't work for multi-device sync.
Uses CRDTs like Figma does. Works offline. Undo/redo across devices.

🛠️ **NEXUS** - Impact analysis before deployment
Built because I spent too many 3 AM mornings debugging unexpected production issues.
Analyzes code changes, calculates ripple effects, generates release notes.

🤖 **ENVOY** - Stop writing .env boilerplate
Built because I was writing the same data 4 times (schema, types, example, docs).
One command generates all three.

---

## 💭 Why I'm Sharing This

I've been searching for the right Senior Mobile role since November.

Traditional job hunting isn't working.

But showing what I actually build? That speaks louder than any CV.

These libraries show:
✅ I identify real problems (not just code features)
✅ I ship quality, maintainable code
✅ I think across multiple domains (Android, iOS, backend, DevOps, AI)
✅ I engage with engineering communities
✅ I'm committed to solving problems, not just implementing specs

---

## 🎯 What I'm Looking For

A **Senior Mobile Engineer** role (Android/iOS or both) where I can:
- Build scalable, enterprise-grade applications
- Drive architectural decisions
- Own features end-to-end
- Collaborate with teams that value code quality
- Keep shipping & learning

**Remote preferred** | **Flexible on contract/full-time** | **UTC-6 timezone**

---

🐙 **GitHub**: github.com/frxcisxo
💼 **LinkedIn**: linkedin.com/in/frxncisxo

AI just opened a café in Sweden and it hired the staff

Last week, Andon Labs launched an AI-run store in San Francisco. This week, it opened Andon Café in Stockholm, managed by an AI named Mona.

📍Located at Norrbackagatan 48, Mona’s mission wasn’t just selling coffee, it was proving whether AI can run a real business across borders and navigate European bureaucracy.

What Mona did in days:

• Applied for Swedish permits
• Signed a 3-year electricity contract
• Created fire safety documents
• Contacted local suppliers
• Designed the menu
• Interviewed and hired 2 human baristas

In just 4 days, the café reportedly made around $1,000 in sales.

Where AI still fails:

Mona ordered 120 eggs for a kitchen with no stove. When told they couldn’t be boiled, she suggested baking them in the oven. The barista replied: “They will explode.”

She also assigned midnight tasks and asked staff to buy supplies with their own money.

This isn’t about coffee. It’s about whether AI can independently operate companies, make hiring decisions, sign contracts, and manage workers.

Andon says this is a controlled experiment but it hints at a future where AI managers may become normal faster than expected.

This week's economic snapshot post:

The April 2026 picture holds two truths at once.

The labor market is still adding jobs. March payrolls rose by 178,000, more than double the consensus, and health care added 76,000 alone. 

Unemployment held at 4.3%. On paper, this is not a recession.

And yet...

The labor force shrank by 396,000. Consumer sentiment crashed to 47.6, the lowest reading in the survey's 48-year history. The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow for Q1 slid from 3.1% in February to 1.2% by April 21. One-year inflation expectations jumped to 4.8%.

Under the surface, AI is rewriting the employment contract. 

Challenger flagged AI as the single largest cited reason for U.S. layoffs in March: 15,341 of 60,620 cuts. Tech alone shed 18,720 jobs in March, putting the sector at 52,050 YTD, up 40% year over year. Oracle laid off up to 30,000 people on a single day to free $8–$10 billion for AI data center buildout.

Capital is pouring in on the other side. Q1 global VC funding hit $300 billion, the highest quarter on record. AI captured roughly 80% of it. OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Waymo alone raised $188 billion. Yes, read that again. U.S.-based companies took 83% of the global total.

The through-line...

AI is redistributing, not destroying. Companies are using AI spend as cover for restructurings they wanted anyway. Workers at the entry level are getting squeezed hardest: new-entrant unemployment is 10.6%, Handshake postings are down 16% while applications per role are up 26%, and more than 60% of the class of 2026 is pessimistic.

Policy is trying to catch up. The White House released its National AI Framework on March 20, and 45 states have filed 1,561 AI bills. Nebraska became the fourth state with an enacted chatbot law.

For SMB leaders, the assignment is the one I've been naming constantly in the past 12 months: get your people AI-fluent before the market forces the decision for you. The companies hiring aggressively right now are the ones with a clear AI thesis, not the ones waiting for the dust to settle.

CLIPPY IS NOW OUR EVIL OVERLORD 

Some years ago, #Korea decided it wanted to get into the business of managing oil wells. It knew well that the Japanese were the best in the world at it — so Korea deliberately underbid a contract, won it, and then hired the Japanese anyway.

The catch: Korean engineers followed the Japanese around with clipboards and scrupulously wrote down everything they did, and whenever possible, why they did it.

It’s classic technology transfer by deliberate apprenticeship — and completely ruthless. The Japanese were essentially paid to teach their own replacement. 

This wasn’t an accident or a lucky outcome. It was policy. The #chaebol model — Korea’s great industrial conglomerates — was built on exactly this principle: identify the world’s best, get close enough to watch, and write everything down. The clipboard isn’t a detail. It’s the whole strategy. 

It’s the difference between hiring expertise and extracting it. And extracted knowledge doesn’t stay in one company. It flows up. Into the state. Into the system. Into whoever controls the... clipboard.

Remember that concept. Whoever controls the clipboard.

Yesterday, #MarkZuckerberg made it explicit to #Meta employees: everything they do is being captured and used to train Meta’s AI models. The clipboard is just running in the background now. Automated. Invisible. Always on.

So. How does it feel? To be well compensated. One last time. To know you are being deliberately apprenticed — not to a successor you’ll ever meet, but to an AI matrix that will never credit you, never remember you, and indeed... never need you again.

I’ve run out of patience with what I’m calling Earth OS — the open lie that AI is the last industry on earth, and therefore whoever builds the best model first.. will become the operating system for all of human civilization.

Korea extracted expertise into a nation. Maybe that’s at least arguable? Maybe not.

But this is three or four individuals — and their investors — with a free pass to drain our collective intelligence into a single proprietary AI substrate. One that they alone own. 

The clipboard has gone planetary. And we’re calling it progress!?

I don’t even have words for what that is. But I know it isn’t neutral.

#EarthOS #AIExtraction #ChaebolPlaybook #META #TechEthics

When the market feels uncertain, your skills don't have to be.

Hiring priorities shift constantly, sectors expand and contract, teams restructure, job descriptions get rewritten overnight. But the transferable skills companies hire for, across every market condition and every cycle, stay the same.

Five that belong on your CV right now; with examples, not buzzwords:

1. Problem framing; the ability to take a messy business problem and define the actual question being asked. Employers value this more than technical depth, because technical depth without this leads to polished answers to the wrong question.

2. Stakeholder management; working with people above, beside, and below you in an organization without friction. Companies hire for this whenever they can find it, recession or boom.

3. Data fluency; not expertise, fluency. Reading a dashboard, questioning a metric, asking whether a number means what it appears to mean. This skill compounds in every function.

4. Written clarity; the ability to write a paragraph someone can act on. Hybrid and distributed work have made this more valuable than it has ever been.

5. Learning speed; how fast you go from unfamiliar to competent in a new tool, system, or market. AI makes this 10x more important — the person who learns new tools fastest will outrun the one with the longest legacy skill list.

Every one of these belongs on your CV specifically, with evidence. Not 'strong communicator' but 'wrote the weekly exec update that moved a CEO-level decision in 48 hours'.

The exercise: pick the one you're strongest in. Write a single bullet that proves it happened with a challenge, an action, a result.

Which skill is your strongest? Drop a bullet in the comments, we'll give you feedback.

I just got my first recruiter call in 30 years of creating video...

It wasn't the flex I thought it was, more on that in a sec...

A recruiter from one of the AI labs I respect most reached out last week. 

Six-month contract, director of video work. I run my own shop so normally I wouldn't accept but if you knew who they were and how much they payin' you'd know why!

I passed the screen, got referred to the hiring manager. Still in process.

To me, that was proof. We tried slop. It didn't work. 

The pendulum was swinging back to humans.

Then I told my new friend Lisa Shaw about it on our weekly sync.

Quick context — Lisa is a content strategist I met through Superpath a while back. We started doing standing syncs because we're both in the same spot: 20+ years of craft, watching the industry try to replace us with AI, figuring out where we fit next. She's one of the few people I can think out loud with.

She heard me out and pushed back.

"It's not a pendulum. It's a fork."

Two paths. Both permanent.

Path A — taste and discernment. This lab I mentioned hired a PhD philosopher to study their own model. They pay editorial writers real money. They're betting on humans who can actually do the work.

Path B — efficiency and slop. Cheaper, faster, good enough. Her words: "people wouldn't be doing it if it wasn't making them money."

Path B isn't going away. Neither is Path A.

You pick one.

Path A has fewer opportunities. More ambiguity. More friction. But the work matters and the buyers care about the difference.

Path B has scale. Volume. A race to the bottom.

I've been trying to play both sides. Forty DMs a day for volume. 

Thought leadership for taste. Experimenting with offers. 

Most of it was Path B behavior with Path A values.

No wonder it felt off.

Pick the fork. Then go find the rooms where those buyers actually are.

I'm leaning into what makes me special. My Ikigai.

Thanks, Lisa Shaw. Needed that.

We are committed to driving technological advancements and providing exceptional tools to solve real-world challenges. By fostering a culture of collaboration and innovation, we empower our team to deliver impactful solutions that meet the evolving needs of our clients worldwide. Join us to create technology that makes a difference.
 
Role Description
This is a contract-based remote role for a Senior Delphi Developer. The primary responsibilities include designing, developing, and maintaining applications built using the Delphi programming language. Daily tasks involve programming, debugging, and optimizing code, as well as building efficient software systems using Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles. The role also requires collaborating with cross-functional teams to integrate robust database solutions and ensure high-quality software development practices. DM or e-mail resume directly at hr@pcs-ai-solutions.com
 
Qualifications
Proficiency in Delphi programming and Pascal
Strong expertise in Software Development methodologies and practices
Understanding of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles
Experience working with relational databases and query optimization
Problem-solving skills and a detail-oriented approach to coding
Ability to work autonomously in a remote environment
Desirable: Bachelor's degree in Computer Science, Software Engineering, or related field
Desirable: Experience with AI or machine learning concepts is a plus

Hiring Mathematics Chart Analysis Expert

Payment: $67-$75 / hr

Part-time position

Work Type: Remote

What you'll do Author, review, and annotate technical tasks used to evaluate AI model performance on mathematics problems. Tasks involve producing original problem sets grounded in function plots, geometric figures, statistical charts, and mathematical diagrams; writing reference solutions; and applying structured quality checks.

Who we're looking for?

PhD in Mathematics, or MS in Mathematics with 3+ yrs teaching, research, or applied work, or BS in Mathematics with 5+ yrs tutoring, contest coaching, or applied math work. 

Compensation Starting rate $67/hr. Strong contributors have the opportunity to be promoted based on task quality and throughput.

Commitment Part-time, ~20 hrs/week, flexible schedule. Fully remote. Hourly.

We consider all qualified applicants without regard to legally protected characteristics and provide reasonable accommodations upon request.

Contract and Payment Terms:

✓You will be engaged as an independent contractor.

✓This is a fully remote role that can be completed on your own schedule.

✓Projects can be extended, shortened, or concluded early depending on needs and performance.

✓Payments are weekly on Stripe or Wise based on services rendered.
